meta.title: Pure pipe vs component method in an Angular template
meta.summary: An Angular list template needs to format and derive display values for many rows. Would you use a pure pipe or call a component method from the template?

scenario: You are building an Angular orders table. Each row shows a badge like `Overdue`, `Due soon`, or `On track` based on the order's due date and status. That label is pure display logic and appears in several places: the table, a details summary card, and an export preview. One teammate wants to call a component method such as `getStatusLabel(order)` directly from the template because the logic is already in the component. Another teammate wants to move the display logic into a pure pipe so the transformation is reusable and more explicit in the template. In the interview, the panel asks which direction you would defend.
prompt: Which direction would you defend for this prompt, and how would you explain when the other option is still the better answer?

options.label: Use a pure pipe
options.summary: Fits best when the template needs a pure, reusable display transformation from input values to rendered output.
whenItWins: The logic is a pure transformation of input data into a display value and does not need component side effects.
The same display rule appears in several templates or repeated rows, so reuse and template readability matter.
You want the template to say clearly that this is formatting or derivation logic, not component workflow behavior.
watchOutFor: A pipe is not a good home for logic that depends on mutable hidden state, imperative side effects, or workflow coordination.
A weak answer says pipes are always faster without first checking whether the real issue is simply keeping heavy work out of hot template paths.

options.label: Call a component method from the template
options.summary: Fits best when the logic is tiny, very local to one component, or not really a reusable display transformation.
whenItWins: The method is a small local helper and the logic does not need to be reused across templates.
The behavior is tied closely to component-specific context rather than a pure input-to-output display transform.
The template call is simple and the method is not hiding expensive work behind every change-detection pass.
watchOutFor: Template method calls can become noisy when they hide repeated work on large lists or mix display formatting with component behavior.
A weak answer uses a component method by habit even when the logic is really a reusable pure display rule.

decisionMatrix.title: Pure display transformation
decisionMatrix.prompt: The logic turns input values into a display label or formatted value with no side effects.

cells.note: This is easier to defend because the logic behaves like formatting or derivation, which is exactly what a pure pipe communicates.
It can still work, but now you should explain why keeping it as a method is clearer than a reusable template transform.

decisionMatrix.title: The exact prompt in this battle
decisionMatrix.prompt: A repeated row label is derived from order data and reused in several UI surfaces.

cells.note: This is the cleanest answer because the logic is pure, presentational, and reused across multiple templates.
Possible, but harder to defend because the method keeps a reusable display rule trapped inside one component and can be called repeatedly in the template.

decisionMatrix.title: Small local helper with component-specific context
decisionMatrix.prompt: The logic is tiny, used in one place, and tied closely to component-only state or behavior.

cells.note: Still possible, but it may add extra structure if the transformation is not really a reusable presentation rule.
This becomes easier to defend when the logic is truly local and not a reusable template concern.

evaluationDimensions.title: Is this a pure input-to-output transformation?
evaluationDimensions.description: This is the first question. If the logic is purely derived from its inputs, it starts to look more like a pipe than component behavior.

evaluationDimensions.title: Will the display rule appear in several templates?
evaluationDimensions.description: Reusable display logic often benefits from a pipe because the transformation becomes sharable and easier to spot in templates.

evaluationDimensions.title: How often will the template execute this logic?
evaluationDimensions.description: A strong answer should notice when a method call sits inside a repeated list or hot template path where unnecessary recomputation becomes more expensive.

evaluationDimensions.title: Is this presentation logic or component workflow logic?
evaluationDimensions.description: Pipes are strongest for presentation transforms. Methods are more reasonable when the logic is owned by component behavior instead.

evaluationDimensions.title: Would a pipe clarify the design or just add structure?
evaluationDimensions.description: A balanced answer should not create a pipe for every tiny helper. The question is whether the extraction improves readability and boundaries.

strongAnswer.title: A strong answer here would lean pure pipe because the prompt describes reusable display logic, not component workflow logic
strongAnswer.summary: This status label is a pure transformation from order data to a display value, and it appears in several templates. That makes a pure pipe the easier answer to defend for the exact prompt.
reasoning: The key clue is that the logic is presentation-oriented and deterministic. Given the same order input, it returns the same label every time.
That makes a pure pipe a better fit than a component method because the template can express the transformation directly and the same rule can be reused in multiple UI surfaces.
I would keep a component method when the logic is tiny, local to one component, or tied to component-specific behavior rather than a reusable display transformation.
strongAnswer.recommendation: For this exact prompt, I would move the status-label logic into a pure pipe and keep component methods for local helpers or behavior that belongs to the component itself rather than to reusable presentation logic.

interviewerPushback.question: Are component methods in templates always bad?
interviewerPushback.answer: No. A tiny local helper can be perfectly fine. The problem is when repeated template method calls start hiding reusable display logic or doing more work than the template path needs.

interviewerPushback.question: When would a component method clearly be the better answer?
interviewerPushback.answer: When the logic is small, local to one component, and not really a reusable pure display transformation. It can also make more sense when the behavior depends on component-specific context rather than plain inputs.

interviewerPushback.question: What would change your answer?
interviewerPushback.answer: If the label logic stopped being a reusable pure transform and became tightly coupled to one component's private state or workflow, I would be more comfortable keeping it as a component method.

answerExamples.title: Weak answer
answerExamples.answer: I would use a pipe because methods in templates are bad for performance.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: It does not. This sounds dogmatic and skips the real boundary between reusable pure display logic and small local helpers.

answerExamples.title: Decent answer
answerExamples.answer: I would probably use a pipe because the label is used in multiple places and should stay reusable.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: This is directionally fine, but it still needs a sharper explanation of purity, template hot paths, and when a method is still acceptable.

answerExamples.title: Strong answer
answerExamples.answer: For this prompt I would use a pure pipe because the status label is a pure display transformation from order data to a rendered value, and the same rule is reused in several templates. That makes the transformation easier to see and reuse than a component method buried in one component. I would still keep a component method for tiny local helpers or logic that belongs to the component's own behavior rather than to reusable presentation formatting.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: This sounds strong because it starts from the nature of the logic, not from a blanket performance slogan, and it still leaves room for small local methods where a pipe would add unnecessary structure.

answerFramework: Start by asking whether the logic is a pure presentation transform or component-specific behavior.
Check whether the same rule appears in repeated template paths or multiple templates.
Choose the abstraction that matches that boundary.
Explain the main cost of the other option, either hiding reusable display logic in methods or adding unnecessary pipe structure for tiny local helpers.
End with a simple rule of thumb, for example: pure pipe for reusable pure display transforms, component method for small local behavior.
antiPatterns: Saying template methods are always bad.
Creating a pipe for every tiny helper without checking whether reuse or presentation boundaries actually justify it.
Keeping reusable pure display logic trapped in one component method by default.
Talking only about performance and ignoring readability and ownership.
